How the AI Job Agent Works
01

The Big Picture

Before touching a single line of code, let's understand what this agent actually does — and why it exists.

Your personal hiring consultant. At superhuman speed.

You find a job listing. It looks promising — but between reading the job description, checking your resume for matching skills, writing a tailored cover letter, and guessing which ATS keywords might get you past the filter — you're looking at 30–45 minutes of focused work. For every application.

This project is an AI agent that compresses that entire process into seconds. You paste in a job URL or a block of text, and it hands you back everything you need to apply confidently.

💡 The Core Promise
One input (a job URL or text) → five structured outputs ready for your application. The agent does the reading, matching, writing, and gap-spotting so you can focus on the parts only a human can do.

The 30-minute problem every job seeker knows

Manual application prep isn't hard — it's just slow and repetitive. The same four steps, executed with full concentration, for every single role. This is exactly the kind of structured, repeatable work that software can automate.

1

Read & Parse the Job Description

Identify required skills, nice-to-haves, seniority level, and company culture signals buried in corporate jargon. Takes 5–10 minutes.

2

Match Skills Against Your Resume

Cross-reference every requirement with your background. Figure out your honest match percentage and which gaps are dealbreakers. Takes 10 minutes.

3

Write a Tailored Cover Letter

Draft something that directly addresses the role's priorities — not a generic template. Takes 10–15 minutes to get right.

4

Guess the ATS Keywords

Reverse-engineer which exact phrases the employer's screening software is looking for. Mostly guesswork without a tool. Takes 5 minutes.

⏱ Do the math
Applying to 20 jobs = up to 15 hours of prep work. The agent handles steps 1–4 in under 30 seconds, every time.

Five outputs. One request.

Every time you run the agent against a job posting, it returns five structured pieces of analysis. Each one is designed to be immediately useful — not raw AI output you have to interpret.

①

Match Score

A percentage showing how well your current resume aligns with this specific role's requirements. Helps you decide whether to invest time applying.

②

Skill Comparison Table

A side-by-side view of every required skill versus your demonstrated experience. Green = covered, amber = partial, red = gap.

③

Gap Analysis

Plain-language explanation of what's missing and how serious each gap is — with suggestions for how to address or frame them.

④

Cover Letter Draft

A fully written, role-specific cover letter that speaks directly to the job's stated priorities using language from the description itself.

⑤

ATS Keywords

The exact phrases and terms from the job description that ATS filters are most likely scanning for — ready to weave into your resume.

What a real session looks like

The agent runs from the command line. You give it a job URL or paste in raw text. Within seconds, it replies with the full analysis. Here's that conversation, step by step.

U
You

python main.py analyze --url https://jobs.example.com/senior-data-engineer

A
Agent

Fetching job description from URL...

A
Agent

Loading your candidate profile and selecting the best cover letter template...

A
Agent

Analysing job opportunity with Claude...

✓
Agent — Results Ready

Match Score: 78%
5 required skills matched · 2 gaps identified (Spark, dbt)
Cover letter drafted · 12 ATS keywords extracted
Files saved to: output/senior-data-engineer/

A
🗂 Output files, not just terminal text
The agent saves every result as Markdown and JSON files in a named output folder — so you can review, edit, and reuse the analysis at any time.

The entry point: where your command becomes action

When you run python main.py analyze, Python calls a function named cmd_analyze. This is the very first piece of real code that runs. Let's read it line by line.

CODE 

def cmd_analyze(args):
    if args.jd_file:
        jd_file = Path(args.jd_file)
        message = jd_file.read_text(encoding="utf-8")
    else:
        message = args.input
print("\nAnalysing job opportunity...\n")
summary, files = handle_user_request(message)
print(summary)
PLAIN ENGLISH

def cmd_analyze(args): — Define the function that runs when you type the analyze command. It receives your flags and arguments as args.

if args.jd_file: — Did you pass a --jd-file flag pointing to a local text file? If yes, read that file's contents.

else: message = args.input — Otherwise, the job description came in as direct text (e.g. from a URL that was already fetched, or pasted inline).

print("Analysing...") — Show a progress message in the terminal so you know it's working.

handle_user_request(message) — Hand off the job description text to the main orchestrator. This one call triggers the entire analysis pipeline.

print(summary) — Display the final human-readable summary in the terminal when it's done.

🔀 Two input modes, one pipeline
Notice the if/else: whether you pass a file path or raw text, both paths produce the same message variable. The rest of the code doesn't need to care about where the text came from — a clean design pattern called input normalization.

Check your understanding

Your friend just found a job posting for a role they're excited about. They've heard about this agent and want to try it. Based on what you've learned, let's make sure the expectations are set correctly.

Your friend pastes in a job URL and runs the agent. What should they expect to get back?

Looking at the code, what does cmd_analyze do before calling handle_user_request?

02

Meet the Cast

Every great film has a director, actors, a scriptwriter, and stage crew. This agent has the same four roles — and understanding who does what unlocks the whole codebase.

Four layers. One clean separation of concerns.

The agent is built in four distinct layers, each with a single clear job. This isn't accidental — it's a deliberate architecture decision that makes the system easier to test, maintain, and swap out piece by piece.

Data flows top to bottom: your input enters at layer one, travels through the middle two layers where all the intelligence happens, and exits as finished files at layer four. No layer skips over another.

1

User Input — the Audience

A job URL, a block of pasted text, or a local file. This is the raw material that starts everything. The layer's only job is to get text into the system.

2

Agent Orchestration — the Director

The orchestrator calls every other layer in sequence. It decides what happens next but delegates the actual work.

3

LLM Reasoning — the Scriptwriter

The only layer that touches the LLM. It takes a carefully built prompt and returns structured analysis. One function in, structured data out.

4

Tools Layer — the Stage Crew

Five independent utilities that do concrete tasks: fetch a web page, load a file, parse JSON, write an output file. They never call the LLM — they just move data around.

The Director: handle_user_request

The orchestrator is the code equivalent of a film director — it never acts, but it controls who acts, when, and with what information. The function handle_user_request is exactly 20 lines long, yet it coordinates the entire analysis pipeline.

Read the code on the left and the plain-English translation on the right. Notice how each line calls another function — the orchestrator never does the real work itself.

CODE 

def handle_user_request(message):
    parsed = parse_intent(message)
    intent = parsed.intent
    if intent == Intent.ANALYZE_URL:
        job_url = parsed.url
        jd_text = fetch_job_from_url(job_url)
    candidate = load_candidate()
    candidate_profile = format_candidate_for_prompt(candidate)
    template_opening, focus_areas = select_template(jd_text)
    markdown_section, json_data = call_claude(
        jd_text=jd_text,
        candidate_profile=candidate_profile,
        template_opening=template_opening,
        focus_areas=focus_areas,
    )
PLAIN ENGLISH

parse_intent(message) — Figure out what the user wants. Did they give a URL? Raw text? A file? Returns a structured object with the intent type and extracted data.

if intent == ANALYZE_URL: — If it's a URL, go fetch the actual job description text from the web. This calls a tool — the orchestrator doesn't do the fetching itself.

load_candidate() — Read the candidate's resume and profile from local files. Again, delegated to a tool function.

format_candidate_for_prompt() — Convert the raw profile data into a clean text block that Claude can read inside a prompt.

select_template(jd_text) — Choose the best cover letter template and set of focus areas based on what kind of role this is.

call_claude(...) — Send everything to the LLM layer and get back a Markdown analysis section plus structured JSON data.

🎬 The director's rule
The orchestrator calls things. It never fetches URLs, reads files, or writes prompts directly. Every one of those jobs belongs to a dedicated layer. This is what makes each part independently testable.

The Stage Crew: five tools that do the grunt work

The tools layer is the most underappreciated part of the architecture. These are pure utility functions — they have no opinions, no AI, no side effects beyond what they're told to do. They can be called from anywhere and tested in complete isolation.

Critically, none of these tools touch the LLM. That boundary is enforced by design. If you ever see an LLM call inside a tool function, something has gone wrong.

🌐

fetch_job_from_url

Sends an HTTP request to a job posting URL, strips HTML tags, and returns clean plain text. Handles timeouts and redirects. The LLM never sees raw HTML.

👤

load_candidate

Reads the candidate's YAML or JSON profile from disk. Returns a structured Python object. If the file is missing, it raises a clear error instead of silently failing.

🧩

select_template

Inspects the job description for role-type signals (engineering, product, design, etc.) and returns the matching cover letter template and a list of focus areas for the prompt.

📝

format_candidate_for_prompt

Converts raw profile data into a consistently formatted text block. Ensures the LLM always sees candidate info in the same structure, regardless of what format the source file uses.

💾

save_output_files

Writes the analysis results as Markdown and JSON files into a named output directory. Handles directory creation, filename sanitization, and encoding. Pure I/O, no logic.

The Scriptwriter: call_claude

There is exactly one place in the entire codebase where the API is called. The function call_claude is a thin, focused wrapper around the Claude SDK. Its job is to build the prompt, fire the request, and parse the response — nothing else.

CODE 

def call_claude(jd_text, candidate_profile,
               template_opening, focus_areas):
    client = get_client()
    user_message = build_analysis_prompt(
        jd_text, candidate_profile,
        template_opening, focus_areas
    )
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-6",
        max_tokens=4096,
        system=SYSTEM_PROMPT,
        messages=[{"role": "user",
                   "content": user_message}],
    )
    raw_text = response.content[0].text
    return _parse_response(raw_text)
PLAIN ENGLISH

get_client() — Create (or retrieve) the Anthropic SDK client, which handles authentication with your API key.

build_analysis_prompt(...) — Assemble all the inputs into a single, carefully structured prompt string. This is where the "ask" to Claude is written.

client.messages.create(...) — Send the prompt to Claude's API. Specify the model (claude-opus-4-6), the maximum response length, the system prompt, and the user message.

system=SYSTEM_PROMPT — A persistent set of instructions given to Claude before every user message — defines its role, tone, and output format requirements.

max_tokens=4096 — Cap the response at 4096 tokens to control cost and prevent runaway output.

_parse_response(raw_text) — Extract the structured Markdown and JSON from Claude's raw text output, splitting them into separate return values.

The "Swap Test": why this architecture is special

Here's a question that reveals everything about a codebase's design quality: If you needed to replace Claude with GPT-4 tomorrow, how much code would you have to change?

In this agent, the answer is: exactly one function. Because call_claude is the only place the LLM is referenced, you could swap the entire AI provider by rewriting that single function — and everything else (the orchestrator, all five tools, the CLI entry point) would continue working without a single edit.

🔌 The power of the single LLM boundary
When LLM calls are scattered throughout a codebase, every refactor becomes a risky archaeological dig. Centralizing them in one place means upgrades, cost monitoring, retry logic, and provider changes all happen in one location.

The same principle applies to the tools layer. Each tool is a standalone function with no dependencies on any other tool. You can rewrite fetch_job_from_url to use a different HTTP library, or swap load_candidate to read from a database instead of a file — and nothing else breaks. This is called loose coupling, and it's one of the most valuable properties a codebase can have.

🎭 Back to the film metaphor
If your scriptwriter quits mid-production, a well-structured film crew can hire a new one and hand over the brief. If the stage crew is tangled up with the scriptwriter's personal process — you're in trouble. Clean layers = survivable personnel changes.

Check your understanding

Two questions that test whether you've internalized the layer boundaries — not just memorized the labels.

The agent needs to download a job posting from a URL. Which layer should handle this?

If the Claude API went down permanently, which parts of the system would still work unchanged?

03

The Data Highway

From URL paste to finished cover letter — trace every handoff in the pipeline.

When you paste a URL, what happens in the next 20 seconds?

Think of the system as a postal sorting facility. Mail arrives (your job posting URL), gets read and stamped (intent detection), routed to the right department (fetcher, cleaner, AI engine), and eventually delivered as a polished package (cover letter + JSON summary). Nothing happens by magic — every step has a specific job and hands off to the next worker with a clean baton.

The full journey touches six distinct components, crosses three file-system boundaries, and makes exactly one call to the Claude API. Everything else is plain Python doing what Python does best: string manipulation, HTTP requests, and file I/O.

📮
The sorting-facility metaphor

Incoming mail = raw user message. Sorter = intent parser. Conveyor = orchestrator. Specialist worker = Claude. Outgoing post = exported files. Each worker only sees what they need — no one reads the whole letter.

The Six-Stage Pipeline

Click Next Step to walk through each handoff. Watch which component lights up and what data travels across the wire.

⌨️
User Input
🔍
Intent Parser
🌐
JD Fetcher
🎛️
Orchestrator
🤖
Claude API
💾
Export
Click "Next Step" to begin

How Does the System Know What You Want?

The intent parser runs a strict priority chain: URL check first, then URL pattern match, then keyword checks, then length. Order matters — a message with both a URL and the word "help" is treated as a URL job, not a help request.

CODE 

def parse_intent(message):
    message = message.strip()
    url_match = _URL_PATTERN.search(message)
    if url_match:
        return ParsedIntent(
            intent=Intent.ANALYZE_URL,
            url=url_match.group(0)
        )
    if _HISTORY_PATTERN.search(message):
        return ParsedIntent(intent=Intent.SHOW_HISTORY)
    if _HELP_PATTERN.search(message) and len(message) < 50:
        return ParsedIntent(intent=Intent.HELP)
    if len(message) >= 100:
        return ParsedIntent(
            intent=Intent.ANALYZE_TEXT,
            jd_text=message
        )
    return ParsedIntent(intent=Intent.UNKNOWN)
PLAIN ENGLISH

Strip whitespace so accidental spaces don't fool the checks.

Run the URL regex. If any URL shape is found, immediately return an ANALYZE_URL intent — URL always wins, no matter what else the message says.

Look for words like "history", "past", "previous". If found, return SHOW_HISTORY.

Look for "help" or "?" — but only if the message is short (under 50 chars). A long message with the word "help" is probably a JD, not a help request.

If the message is 100+ characters and nothing else matched, treat it as a raw job description pasted directly.

If none of the above matched, return UNKNOWN — the bot will ask for clarification.

💡
Why the 100-character threshold?

Real job descriptions are almost never shorter than a tweet. The 100-char floor keeps the system from treating a short confused message like "analyse senior data role at Google" as a full JD — it falls through to UNKNOWN and prompts the user to provide more.

How the Fetcher Cleans Raw HTML

A raw job-posting page is mostly clutter — navigation menus, cookie banners, footer links. The fetcher uses BeautifulSoup to surgically remove the noise and surface the actual job content — then caps it at 8 000 characters to keep token costs reasonable.

CODE 

def _extract_text(html):
    soup = BeautifulSoup(html, "html.parser")
    for tag in soup(["script", "style",
                    "nav", "footer", "header"]):
        tag.decompose()
    main_content = (
        soup.find("main")
        or soup.find("article")
        or soup.find(id=re.compile(
            r"job|description|content", re.I))
    )
    target = main_content if main_content else soup.body or soup
    lines = [l.strip() for l in
             target.get_text(separator="\n").splitlines()]
    return "\n".join(l for l in lines if l)[:8000]
PLAIN ENGLISH

Parse the raw HTML string into a navigable tree.

Find every <script>, <style>, <nav>, <footer>, and <header> tag and delete them from the tree entirely. This removes JavaScript, CSS, menus, and footer links in one pass.

Try to find the most content-dense element: a <main> tag first, then <article>, then any element whose id contains "job", "description", or "content" (case-insensitive).

If no landmark element was found, fall back to <body> — or the whole document if there is no body.

Extract all visible text, split on newlines, strip each line of whitespace, and drop empty lines. This collapses all the messy HTML into clean paragraphs.

Hard-cap the result at 8 000 characters. Anything beyond that is usually boilerplate or repeated footer content anyway.

Check Your Understanding

Two quick scenarios. Read carefully — the details matter.

Scenario

A user sends the 10-character message: "help me". No URL, no history keyword. What does the intent parser return?

Which intent does the parser assign?

Scenario

The fetcher calls tag.decompose() on every <nav> and <footer> element before extracting text.

Why does the fetcher remove <nav> and <footer> tags?

04

The Clever Tricks

Three elegant engineering patterns that do a lot with very little.

Trick #1 — Keyword Template Selection

Most AI tools reach for a machine-learning model when they need to classify input. This agent does something more honest: it counts keyword matches. Five cover letter templates each have a keyword list. The job description is scanned against every list. The template with the most hits wins. No neural network required.

Simple? Yes. Reliable? Very. The trick is knowing when simple is exactly enough. A large language model would be overkill for a task that boils down to string contains — and it would cost API credits on every single job description scanned.

CODE 

def select_template(jd_text, company="", role=""):
    templates = load_templates()
    jd_lower = jd_text.lower()
    scores = {}
    for template_name, template_data in templates.items():
        if template_name == "general":
            continue
        keywords = template_data.get("keywords", [])
        score = sum(1 for kw in keywords
                   if kw.lower() in jd_lower)
        scores[template_name] = score
    best_template = max(scores, key=scores.get)
    if scores.get(best_template, 0) == 0:
        best_template = "general"
    return (templates[best_template]["opening"],
            templates[best_template]["focus_areas"])
PLAIN ENGLISH

Load all template definitions from disk (JSON files).

Lowercase the whole job description once so every keyword comparison is case-insensitive without repeating the conversion.

Skip the "general" template during scoring — it is the fallback, not a competitor.

For each specialist template, count how many of its keywords appear anywhere in the JD. Store that count.

Pick the template with the highest count.

If every specialist scored zero (no recognisable keywords at all), fall back to the general template rather than returning a random winner.

Return the winning template's opening paragraph and its list of focus areas — both ready to inject into the Claude prompt.

Meet the Five Templates

Each template is a Swiss Army blade: sharp for exactly one job track. The keywords are the blades detection wire — if the JD is thick with them, the template wins.

📊
data_analyst

Keywords: SQL, dashboard, Tableau, Power BI, Excel, KPI, reporting, stakeholder, visualization, metrics. Opens with business-insight framing. Focus areas: storytelling with data, stakeholder communication, reporting tools.

🧪
data_science

Keywords: machine learning, model, Python, statistics, neural, NLP, deep learning, prediction, scikit, feature. Opens with analytical depth. Focus areas: ML pipelines, experimentation, model deployment.

🗺️
geospatial

Keywords: GIS, geospatial, spatial, mapping, ArcGIS, QGIS, remote sensing, GPS, PostGIS, cartography. Opens with location-intelligence framing. Focus areas: spatial analysis, map design, geo-database work.

⚙️
data_engineer

Keywords: pipeline, ETL, Spark, Airflow, data warehouse, dbt, Kafka, cloud, infrastructure, orchestration. Opens with systems-thinking framing. Focus areas: scalable pipelines, data reliability, platform work.

🌐
general

The fallback. Used when no specialist template scores above zero. Generic but professional — covers analytical skills broadly without over-committing to a single track.

🔧
Adding your own template is trivial

Create a new JSON file in the templates directory with an opening string, a focus_areas list, and a keywords list. The selector loop picks it up automatically — no code changes needed.

Trick #2 — Delimiter-Based Prompting

Asking an AI to "return JSON" works — until it adds a friendly preamble, wraps the JSON in a markdown code block, or appends a sign-off. Any of those breaks json.loads(). The solution: tell Claude to wrap each section in named delimiters that a regex can find with 100% reliability.

CODE 

def _parse_response(raw_text):
    md_match = re.search(
        r"===MARKDOWN_START===\n(.*?)\n===MARKDOWN_END===",
        raw_text, re.DOTALL
    )
    markdown_section = md_match.group(1).strip()
    json_match = re.search(
        r"===JSON_START===\n(.*?)\n===JSON_END===",
        raw_text, re.DOTALL
    )
    json_data = json.loads(json_match.group(1).strip())
    return markdown_section, json_data
PLAIN ENGLISH

Search the full response text for everything between ===MARKDOWN_START=== and ===MARKDOWN_END===. The re.DOTALL flag lets the dot match newlines, so multi-line cover letters are captured in one hit.

Extract group(1) — the content inside the markers — and strip any leading or trailing whitespace.

Do the same for the JSON section, between ===JSON_START=== and ===JSON_END===.

Parse the JSON string into a Python dict. Because the delimiters guarantee no prose surrounds it, this never fails due to formatting surprises.

Return both the markdown cover letter and the structured JSON analysis as a clean tuple.

⚓
The delimiter guarantee

The system prompt instructs Claude to place its cover letter between the MARKDOWN markers and its analysis between the JSON markers. No post-processing, no stripping of backticks, no guessing where one section ends and the other begins. The delimiters are load-bearing — they are what makes the whole output pipeline deterministic.

Trick #3 — Compact Candidate Formatting

The candidate profile — skills, experience, education — gets sent to Claude on every single request. If it were sent as a verbose paragraph or a full JSON object, that could easily consume 1 000–2 000 tokens of context window before the job description even appears.

The smart move: format the candidate data as a compact, structured plain-text block — key: value pairs on single lines, skills as comma-separated lists, no nested JSON. This typically fits a rich profile into under 400 tokens while still giving Claude everything it needs to personalise the output.

💸
Why token efficiency is a design concern, not a micro-optimisation

At typical API rates, every 1 000 tokens of input costs a small fraction of a cent. Send 100 applications a month and those fractions add up. More importantly, a bloated prompt pushes the actual job description further from the top of the context — and recent research suggests models pay more attention to content near the start and end of long prompts. Compact formatting is both cheaper and more effective.

1

Candidate profile loaded from candidate.json

→
2

Formatted into compact key:value plain text

→
3

Concatenated with JD text + template + delimiters

→
4

Single API call — minimal tokens, maximum signal

Check Your Understanding

Two quick questions. Pick the best answer for each.

Scenario

A job description mentions: SQL, dashboard, Tableau, and KPI. No mentions of machine learning, Spark, or GIS.

Which template does the selector pick?

Why use delimiter markers instead of just asking Claude to return JSON?

05

Going Live — The Feishu Bot

From terminal tool to team-wide chat integration

The agent works great in the terminal. You type a job URL, you get a polished analysis back. But what if your whole team wants to use it — without opening a laptop, without touching a command line? That's where Feishu comes in.

Feishu lets you build a bot that lives right inside a group chat. A teammate pastes a job link, the bot replies with a full analysis. No terminal, no setup — just a message. The magic that makes this possible is a webhook: Feishu delivers every incoming message to your server as an HTTP POST request.

The Flask server in app/channels/feishu_bot.py is the receptionist at the front desk — it greets every incoming webhook, hands off the real work to colleagues in the back office, and keeps the door open for the next visitor.

What is Feishu/Lark?
Feishu is ByteDance's enterprise collaboration platform, dominant in Chinese tech companies. Outside China it's called Lark. The same codebase, the same API. If you've used Slack or Teams, the mental model is identical — the only difference is the package name: lark_oapi.

The 3-Second Problem

Here's the catch. When Feishu sends a webhook to your server, it expects a response within 3 seconds. If your server takes longer, Feishu assumes something went wrong and retries — sending the same message again and again. Your agent calls Claude, parses a job description, writes a cover letter. That takes 15–20 seconds.

Do the maths: 15 seconds > 3 seconds. If you try to do all the work before replying, every single message triggers a cascade of retries. The user ends up with five identical analyses in their chat. Not great.

The Insight: Acknowledge First, Work Later
The solution is to split the response from the work. The Flask route returns {"msg": "ok"} with a 200 status the moment the webhook arrives — before any analysis happens. The heavy lifting is handed off to a background thread that starts simultaneously and sends the real reply whenever it's ready.

Think of it like a receptionist at a busy office. When a visitor arrives, the receptionist doesn't make them stand at the door while the whole office scrambles to prepare. She says "Welcome, I'll let the team know you're here" — and the visitor is seated immediately. The colleagues in the back get the message and do the actual work at their own pace.

The Full Interaction Flow

Watch the webhook travel through the system

F
Feishu Server

POST /feishu/webhook
{"event_type": "im.message.receive_v1", "content": "analyze https://jobs.example.com/senior-pm"}

W
Flask (Webhook Handler)

Got it! Returning {"msg": "ok"} with HTTP 200 — right now, before any processing. Feishu won't retry.

W
Flask (to Background Thread)

Hey, I've started a daemon thread for you — process this job URL: https://jobs.example.com/senior-pm. I'll keep the front door open.

T
Background Thread

On it! Sending an acknowledgment to the user in Feishu chat now: "Analysing job opportunity, please wait..."

T
Background Thread (thinking...)

Calling handle_user_request()... fetching the JD... calling Claude... parsing the structured output... generating cover letter... this takes about 15 seconds, but nobody's waiting at the front door.

T
Background Thread

Done! Sending the full analysis back to the user via _send_reply(message_id, summary). The user sees match score, skill table, and cover letter — all in the Feishu thread.

?

The Webhook Handler, Decoded

Every line has a reason

CODE 

@app.route("/feishu/webhook", methods=["POST"])
def webhook():
    body = request.get_json(silent=True) or {}
    if body.get("type") == "url_verification":
        challenge = body.get("challenge", "")
        return jsonify({"challenge": challenge})
    event_type = body.get("header", {}).get("event_type", "")
    if event_type == "im.message.receive_v1":
        thread = threading.Thread(
            target=_process_message,
            args=(body,),
            daemon=True
        )
        thread.start()
    return jsonify({"msg": "ok"})
PLAIN ENGLISH

Line 1–2: Register this function as the handler for any POST request arriving at /feishu/webhook.

Line 3: Parse the incoming JSON body. If it's malformed, default to an empty dict instead of crashing.

Lines 4–6: Handle the URL verification challenge — a one-time setup handshake Feishu requires to confirm you own the server.

Line 7: Extract the event type from the nested header. Feishu wraps metadata in a header object.

Lines 8–13: If it's a new incoming message, spin up a daemon thread pointing at _process_message and start it immediately.

Line 14: Return 200 OK right away — regardless of how long the background thread takes. Feishu is satisfied and won't retry.

One Agent, Three Interfaces

The power of decoupled architecture

Here's something elegant hidden in this codebase. The CLI, the interactive REPL, and the Feishu bot are three completely different front doors — but they all walk into the same room. Every single interface ultimately calls one function: handle_user_request() in app/agent/orchestrator.py.

This is decoupled architecture in practice. The interfaces know nothing about Claude, cover letters, or JSON parsing. The orchestrator knows nothing about Feishu message IDs, terminal ANSI colours, or argparse arguments. They only share one contract: pass in a string, get back a summary string and an optional list of saved files.

⌨️

CLI

app/main.py
argparse parses flags → calls handle_user_request() → prints result to terminal

python -m app.main analyze "url"
💬

Interactive REPL

cmd_interactive() in main.py
REPL loop reads stdin → calls handle_user_request() → prints result, loops

python -m app.main interactive
🤖

Feishu Bot

app/channels/feishu_bot.py
Webhook → Flask → daemon thread → calls handle_user_request() → replies via lark_oapi

python -m app.channels.feishu_bot
Why This Pattern Matters
Want to add a Slack bot tomorrow? A web API? A mobile app? You write the interface adapter — roughly 50 lines — and the entire agent brain comes for free. The handle_user_request() function is your stable contract. Build around it, not inside it.

You've Built It End to End

You now understand a complete AI agent system — from user input to LLM reasoning to structured output, deployed across multiple interfaces. In five modules you've traced the full journey: a user types a job URL, the intent parser classifies it, the orchestrator coordinates the workflow, Claude reasons over the data, and a structured result lands in a terminal, a REPL, or a Feishu chat — all from the same underlying logic.

Key Takeaways from This Course
  • 01 Intent parsing decouples input from action. The system understands your intent before deciding what to do — no rigid command syntax required.
  • 02 Structured prompts produce structured outputs. Pydantic schemas enforce that Claude returns parseable, typed data — not freeform prose.
  • 03 Threading solves real-time constraints. Any time you integrate with a system that has a timeout, the acknowledge-then-process pattern is your friend.
  • 04 One core function, many interfaces. handle_user_request() is the system's stable contract. The CLI, REPL, and Feishu bot are just different front doors into the same room.

Final Check

Feishu sends a webhook. The agent analysis takes 20 seconds. What happens if you don't use threading?

All three interfaces — CLI, REPL, and Feishu bot — share the same core function. What is it called?
